nativeRange.habitat: The Marimo is native to cold to cool freshwater lakes with low-to-moderate biological activity and moderate-to-high calcium content. The distinctive spherical shape develops naturally where gentle wave action gradually rolls free-floating algal filaments along the lake bed, slowly compacting them into a dense ball over many years.

decorPreferences: rests freely on substrate (sand or fine gravel) where it can be rolled and reshaped
no anchoring needed — the ball is free-living, not attached
gentle flow helps keep all sides exposed to light and prevents detritus accumulation
best placed where it gets indirect light and is easy to rotate during water changes
can also be fragmented and tied to driftwood or rock to form a carpet

goodWith: dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) — graze biofilm and detritus from the surface
small peaceful community fish (tetras, rasboras, corydoras)
avoidWith: goldfish (will tear the ball apart and eat fragments)
plant-shredding cichlids (Oscar, severum, large Central American cichlids)
any tank running consistently above ~26 °C (heat stress causes browning and core die-off)

summary: An iconic spherical green alga from cold northern lakes, traded and shelved as an aquarium plant. Generally considered the easiest aquarium 'plant' to keep — tolerates cool water, low light, and needs no CO2 or fertilization, but is sensitive to heat and direct sunlight.
careNotes:
Despite the name, the Marimo Moss Ball is technically NOT a moss or a plant — it is a free-living spherical growth form of the filamentous green alga Aegagropila linnaei, belonging to the order Cladophorales in the family Pithophoraceae. Although strictly an alga, Marimo Moss Balls are sold and shelved alongside aquarium plants in both retail stores and online. A note on naming: as of 2023, the algae taxonomy community reclassified the accepted scientific name to Aegagropila brownii, treating A. linnaei as a synonym; however, the aquarium hobby and most retailers continue to use the older name A. linnaei, which remains the most widely recognized name in hobbyist communication.

Origin and appearance: the Marimo is native to cold-to-cool freshwater lakes in Japan (most famously Lake Akan), Iceland (Lake Mývatn), and parts of northern Europe including Estonia, Scotland, and Austria. The distinctive ball shape develops naturally where gentle wave action gradually rolls free-floating algal filaments along the lake bed, slowly compacting them into a dense sphere over many years. Wild specimens from Lake Akan can reach 20–30 cm across; those from Lake Mývatn top out at around 12 cm. Commercially sold balls are typically 2–5 cm.

Temperature: this is the single most critical parameter for Marimo. It is a COLDWATER species with a tolerance range of 5–25 °C, and it is generally advised to keep it below approximately 26 °C. Prolonged exposure above roughly 27 °C causes browning, core die-off, and eventual disintegration of the ball. Marimo is commonly kept in betta tanks and tropical community aquariums, but it will degrade faster at warm tropical temperatures — often surviving only 1–2 years in a heated tank, compared to decades in its native cold lakes.

Lighting: low to medium. Direct sunlight rapidly browns the surface and should be avoided.

Nutrition: no CO2 injection and no fertilization are needed. Growth is essentially independent of nutrient load.

Growth rate: extremely slow — approximately 5 mm (0.2 in) per year. The ball purchased today will be roughly the same size in a year's time.

Placement: the Marimo rests freely on the substrate and is not anchored or attached to anything. A newly added ball may float temporarily until it becomes waterlogged, then sinks. Rotating the ball a quarter turn at each water change ensures all sides receive light and prevents one face from browning.

Maintenance: at every water change, gently squeeze the ball to release trapped detritus and oxygen bubbles, then reshape it by hand and return it to the tank. If detritus has built up, remove the ball, swish it in dechlorinated water, gently squeeze, and place it back.

INVASIVE-SPECIES / ZEBRA-MUSSEL CONTAMINATION: in early March 2021, zebra mussels (Dreissena polymorpha) — a highly destructive invasive freshwater bivalve — were confirmed attached to and inside commercially sold Marimo Moss Balls first identified at a Seattle retailer. The contamination spread rapidly; by late April 2021 it had been reported across 46 US states. The US Fish and Wildlife Service issued a destroy-don't-dump directive, and large retailers voluntarily recalled affected stock. Several US jurisdictions, including the District of Columbia, temporarily banned Moss Ball sales; most bans were lifted after supply-chain inspection protocols were tightened. As of 2026, Marimo Moss Balls are legal in Michigan and most US states. Nonetheless, all new balls should be inspected carefully for any small bivalve attached to or inside the algae. Used moss balls or water from a Marimo tank should NEVER be released into natural waterways; unwanted balls should be disposed of by freezing or boiling first.

Protected status: the Marimo is a protected species in Japan (since the 1920s) and in Iceland (since 2006); wild collection is illegal in both countries. Almost all commercial Marimo is now produced by reshaping cultivated filamentous Cladophorales stock — primarily from Ukraine and Eastern Europe — rather than harvested from wild populations.

Compatibility notes: goldfish, crayfish, plant-shredding cichlids, and plecos will physically destroy the ball. With peaceful tankmates — dwarf shrimp, snails, small community fish — the Marimo is entirely safe and shrimp will actively graze biofilm from its surface.
breedingNotes: Propagation in the aquarium is by fragmentation only. To divide a healthy ball, gently squeeze out the water, cut it in half (or into smaller pieces) with a knife or scissors, and roll each clump into a new sphere. Tying loosely with cotton sewing thread helps hold the shape until the fragment firms up on its own, though leaving the pieces loose also works. Each fragment continues growing as an independent ball. True sexual reproduction has not been documented in cultivation. Wild ball formation depends on lake-bed wave action gradually compacting free-floating filaments — a process that cannot be replicated in a home aquarium, so the spherical shape must be established and maintained by hand-rolling.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Marimo Moss Ball Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary care guide. Used for: scientific name Aegagropila linnaei, identification as a green alga (not moss/plant), native habitat (cold freshwater lakes; Japan referenced), temperature preference (low-to-medium with successful keeping up to ~80 °F / 27 °C in betta tanks), lighting (low to medium, avoid direct sunlight), extreme slow growth (5 mm / 0.2 in per year), wild size 8–12 in (20–30 cm), commercial size ~2 in or smaller, maintenance (squeeze and rotate during water changes, swish in aquarium water if detritus accumulates), propagation (squeeze, cut in half, roll, tie with thread), shrimp/snail/invertebrate compatibility, and overall 'world's easiest aquarium plant' care positioning. The product page (https://www.aquariumcoop.com/products/marimo-moss-ball) WebFetch failed with a redirect loop; the linked care-guide blog post was used instead and contains the same care information.

additional.name: Tropica — Aegagropila linnaei
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Used for: confirmed scientific name (Aegagropila linnaei, with Cladophora aegagropila as older synonym), care difficulty (Easy), growth rate (Slow), lighting (Low), CO2 (Low — i.e., none required), adult size 3–10 cm (with potential to grow larger), 'cosmopolitan' / shallow-lake origin, decorative free placement on substrate or attached to hardscape, propagation by fragmentation into smaller pieces that re-form spheres over time, and the husbandry note that the ball must be turned regularly to retain its shape. Tropica does not list temperature, pH, or hardness ranges for this species on its product page.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Marimo (Aegagropila linnaei / Aegagropila brownii)
additional.notes: Tertiary source used for: taxonomy (Chlorophyta > Ulvophyceae > Cladophorales > Pithophoraceae), 2023 Guiry & Frödén renaming to Aegagropila brownii (A. linnaei retained as the hobby-canonical name here), native-range detail (Lake Akan Japan; Lake Mývatn Iceland; Lake Zell Austria; Scotland; Estonia), Lake Mývatn colony depth 2–2.5 m used for depthRangeM, wild sizes (Lake Akan 20–30 cm, Lake Mývatn ~12 cm), 5 mm/yr growth rate confirmation, protected status (Japan since 1920s, Iceland since 2006), and the 2021 zebra-mussel contamination incident (detected in 21 US states by 8 March 2021, voluntary major-retailer recalls).

additional.name: U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service — Invasive Zebra Mussels Found in Moss Balls
additional.notes: Authoritative source for the 2021 zebra-mussel contamination incident and the destroy-don't-dump federal guidance. Used to document the invasive-species advisory in careNotes. Several US jurisdictions (including DC) temporarily banned moss ball sales; Michigan kept sales legal but issued a public-awareness alert. Most bans were lifted once supply-chain inspection tightened, but the disposal advisory (freeze or boil before discarding; never flush or release into waterways) remains current as of this writeup.

propagation: fragmentation (cut in half, roll into new balls, optionally tie with cotton thread)
rests freely on substrate — no anchoring needed
can be fragmented and tied to driftwood or rock to form a Cladophora carpet
